author: @Flashahol
text:
I definitely wouldn't use these earbuds on the bus though, but great science project!

This is an electrostatic speaker per se, but I would rather call this arrangement a flat plane capacitive speaker just because it sounds better and may confuse more people. 
On the academic side (if I may be serious for a second): different types of paper may give different results (rice paper?), double sided pencil coating and perhaps cutting excess paper could help amplify the sound? How about an array of speakers on a single sheet?? I must have read something about this when I was a teenager.

Hidden perk of this project: play it loud enough and it becomes a fire starter. 

Still reading? This construction method just looks like magic, it reminds me of the scene where Taylor, in the original Planet of the Apes movie, made the paper airplane and launched it in the air.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I haven't seen that movie in ages - good film. Anyhow you are spot on I think - lots to play around with here to optimise this

author: @hanslepoeter5167
text: You made an electrostatic speaker. I didn't know either but i found a wikipedia page on that.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: in a sense yes but it really generates thrust from the asymmetric capacitance - have a look at the experiments NASA did on asymmetric thrust capacitors as possible EM drives

replies.author: @hanslepoeter5167
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering I indeed found some information on that. That's cool. However I think it's a very different subject. That's not what you made.  You might try to make an EM drive like that. If you get a measurable result it would be a great success.

author: @grahamcustard7716
text:
I have a Masters degree in Acoustics and about 40 years experience in the subject. Never have I seen an Idea of painting your own speakers like this, Well done, I enjoy all your videos, but this one especially!

You've invented a method of injecting a high voltage (and hence a reasonable displacement) onto the paper in response to an electrical sound. This could have enormous potential for anti-noise applications. Many speakers and microphones painted together on a sheet of paper to cancel out the incoming sound ..... noise reducing wallpaper anyone? 😀

@Zodliness I think the design in this video audio is created by heating the air.  I'm not sure that doing it on both sides would benefit. 

On the other hand, two plates of a capacitor can use electrostatic forces to move the plates.
